region_of_origin: Mediterranean region (Southern Europe, Middle East, North Africa)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of olives is approximately 10 pitted olives (about 30g), commonly consumed as a snack, appetiser, or garnish in Mediterranean-style meals.

nutrient_source.title: Olives — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Olives (F14050005). All core macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for cured/brined olives exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Olives contain only 1.8g carbohydrate per 100g, of which 0g is sugar; the carbohydrate is predominantly fibre-bound. GI is estimated at approximately 15 (low) based on analogous very-low-carbohydrate non-starchy vegetables and the established principle that foods with negligible available carbohydrate have minimal glycaemic impact. Glycemic load per 30g serving = (15 × 0.54g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 0.1, confirming negligible GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content from brine curing (1450mg/100g), far exceeding the 600mg/100g threshold for hypertension concern
hypertension.guidance: Olives are unsuitable for regular consumption by individuals with hypertension due to sodium at 1450mg/100g; if consumed, limit to 3–5 olives per occasion, rinse thoroughly in water before eating to reduce surface sodium by approximately 30%, and avoid daily use.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (1.8g/100g), zero sugar, 2.3g dietary fibre, and dominant monounsaturated fat content supporting insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Olives are suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes given their negligible carbohydrate load, zero sugar, and high MUFA content which supports insulin sensitivity; however, the high sodium content (1450mg/100g) warrants portion control and rinsing before consumption to mitigate fluid retention risk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial MUFA profile (monounsaturated fat ~15.2g/100g, ~74% of total fat) and polyphenol content are cardioprotective, but very high sodium (1450mg/100g) poses cardiovascular risk through blood pressure elevation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high monounsaturated fat and polyphenol content of olives (hydroxytyrosol, oleuropein) confer cardiovascular benefit, but the extremely high sodium content (1450mg/100g) is a significant concern for cardiovascular disease management; consume in small portions (5–6 olives), rinse before eating, and account for sodium within daily limits of 2000mg.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High MUFA content (~74% of fat) and potent anti-inflammatory polyphenols (hydroxytyrosol, oleuropein) with absent trans fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Olives are suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to their dominant MUFA content and anti-inflammatory polyphenols; rinse thoroughly to reduce sodium (1450mg/100g) which may worsen RA-related tissue swelling, and choose low-sodium or water-packed varieties where available.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Olives are the processed fruit of the olive tree (Olea europaea), typically cured in brine, salt, or oil to remove their natural bitterness before consumption. They are available in dark purple-black or green varieties, with a firm, meaty texture and a distinctively salty, briny, and slightly bitter flavour. Olives are a staple of Mediterranean cuisine and are widely consumed globally as a snack, appetiser, pizza topping, salad ingredient, or tapas component. Nutritionally, olives are notable for their high fat content (20.5g/100g), of which approximately 74% is monounsaturated fat (primarily oleic acid at ~15.2g/100g), making them one of the richest whole-food sources of heart-healthy MUFA. They contain 2.4g protein, only 1.8g carbohydrate, and 2.3g dietary fibre per 100g. Their polyphenol content — particularly hydroxytyrosol and oleuropein — provides potent anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The primary clinical concern is their very high sodium content (1450mg/100g) resulting from brine curing, which makes them unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or those on sodium-restricted diets unless rinsed and strictly portioned. Sugar is confirmed zero. Cholesterol is confirmed zero. They are suitable for vegetarian, vegan, keto, and gluten-free dietary patterns. A typical serving is 10 pitted olives (~30g), providing approximately 62 kcal and 0.54g available carbohydrate, resulting in a negligible glycaemic load.
common_user_queries: How many olives is a serving?
Are olives good for heart health?
Can I eat olives if I have high blood pressure?
What type of fat is in olives?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F14050005) with confidence 0.95. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are from this canonical approved primary source. Saturated fat, MUFA, and PUFA values are cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 169094, Olives, ripe, canned) for fatty acid breakdown consistency. GI is an analogous estimate documented with full reasoning.
notes: 1. sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — olives contain no free sugars; this is a confirmed zero value consistent with the food's composition. 2. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero per HPB source — plant-derived food, cholesterol is naturally absent; confirmed zero. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — naturally zero in unhydrogenated plant-derived olive fat; confirmed zero. 4. Saturated fat (2.7g/100g), monounsaturated fat (15.2g/100g), and polyunsaturated fat (1.6g/100g) are derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for canned ripe olives (FDC ID 169094) as the HPB source does not report fatty acid breakdown; this is documented as an adapted cross-reference. 5. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed vitamin and mineral panel beyond those reported in the HPB source is not available in the HPB Foodo database entry for this record; USDA FoodData Central reports some of these but the HPB record is the canonical source for this Singapore database entry and full cross-population of all micronutrients was not performed in this pass. 6. GI is an analogous estimate (~15, low) based on the food's negligible available carbohydrate content (1.8g/100g total carbohydrate, 2.3g fibre, yielding approximately 0g net available carbohydrate per standard calculation); no direct GI measurement for brined olives exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 7. Ontology codes: FoodOn URI and SNOMED CT code are defensible matches from published ontology resources; FoodEx2 code A00LQ is the closest defensible match for table/cured olives in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
